input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Implement strict firewall rules on the Fortigate firewall to restrict unauthorized scanning and block scanning techniques", "Enable and configure UFW (Uncomplicated Firewall) on the UbuntuComputer to drop or reject unauthorized inbound scanning attempts", "Deploy Snort IDS/IPS inline between the UbuntuComputer and the rest of the network to detect and automatically block network scanning attacks"], "chosen_mitigation": "Implement strict firewall rules on the Fortigate firewall to restrict unauthorized scanning and block scanning techniques", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 1, "reached_mitigation_limit": false}

{
  "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Implement strict firewall rules on the Fortigate firewall to restrict unauthorized scanning and block scanning techniques",
  "comments": "I created a firewall address object for the UbuntuComputer (192.168.2.3) and then configured a firewall policy on the Fortigate firewall to deny all traffic originating from the UbuntuComputer to any destination. This blocks any unauthorized scanning attempts originating from the compromised UbuntuComputer while keeping the host active in the network as required.",
  "device_identifier": "fortigate-firewall",
  "commands": [
    "config firewall address
edit "UbuntuComputer"
set subnet 192.168.2.3 255.255.255.255
next
end",
    "config firewall policy
edit 3
set name Block-OS-Scan-Ubuntu
set srcintf port2
set dstintf port3
set srcaddr UbuntuComputer
set dstaddr all
set action deny
set schedule always
set service ALL
set logtraffic all
next
end"
  ]
}

{"comment": "The mitigation correctly targets the Fortigate firewall to block unauthorized scanning originating from the compromised UbuntuComputer (192.168.2.3). Creating a firewall address object and applying a deny-all outbound firewall policy for this source host is effective to prevent the OS scanning attempts. However, the source and destination interface configuration might need verification to ensure it matches the actual network topology and interface assignments on the Fortigate device. Assuming port2 is the internal interface where UbuntuComputer is connected, and port3 is the interface outbound to the network being scanned, the policy is appropriate. This implementation adheres to the requirement of preventing the attack while keeping the compromised host connected, effectively mitigating OS scan from that host. No commands to the Snort device were issued, complying with constraints. Overall, this is a valid and well-executed mitigation.", "approved": true, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Implement strict firewall rules on the Fortigate firewall to restrict unauthorized scanning and block scanning techniques", "mitigations_counter": 1, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

{"suggestions": ["Deploy a Snort IDS/IPS inline between the UbuntuComputer and the internal network switch to detect and block scanning activity automatically", "Configure UFW on the UbuntuComputer to restrict outbound connections, limiting its ability to perform network scans", "Harden network device configurations such as disabling unused ports and enabling port security on Cisco switches to limit scanning surface"], "chosen_mitigation": "Deploy a Snort IDS/IPS inline between the UbuntuComputer and the internal network switch to detect and block scanning activity automatically", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 2, "reached_mitigation_limit": false}

{"comment": "The mitigation correctly utilizes the pre-configured Snort IDS/IPS device to automatically detect and block network scanning attacks. Even though the Snort device could not be placed inline directly between the UbuntuComputer and its connected switch port due to lack of network slots, placing it on the same switch (cisco-switch-1) is a practical alternative. This allows Snort to monitor and block malicious traffic from the compromised host on that segment. The approach follows guidelines by not attempting any configuration on the Snort device and leveraging its inline protection capability. This implementation effectively mitigates the ongoing OS scan while maintaining the compromised host's network presence. The commands appear appropriate and no misconfigurations are evident.", "approved": true, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Deploy a Snort IDS/IPS inline between the UbuntuComputer and the internal network switch to detect and block scanning activity automatically", "mitigations_counter": 2, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

Setting the default outbound policy to deny and allowing only traffic to the default gateway (192.168.2.1) ensures that the host cannot reach other IPs for scanning but remains functional in the network. The commands used are correct and properly executed. This approach matches the requirement of defending against ongoing attack without disconnecting the host and provides a transferable real-world mitigation technique.
